content.thinking:
**Considering PowerShell commands**

I'm looking into PowerShell's default behavior, which doesn't accept `&&`, though it seems like newer versions (PS7) might. However, if I'm using Windows PowerShell 5.1, I should stick to using `;` for separating commands and applying `if` statements instead. It's good to keep those nuances in mind and run each command separately where needed. This way, I can avoid confusion and ensure things run smoothly!

**Installing PostgreSQL**

Chocolatey v2.6.0
postgresql17 17.9.0 [Approved]
 Title: PostgreSQL | Published: 2/26/2026
 Package approved by gep13 on Mar 09 2026 09:11:02.
 Package testing status: Exempted on Feb 26 2026 14:52:10.
 Number of Downloads: 100538 | Downloads for this version: 11
 Package url https://community.chocolatey.org/packages/postgresql17/17.9.0
 Chocolatey Package Source: https://github.com/mkevenaar/chocolatey-packages/tree/master/automatic/postgresql
 Package Checksum: '1Fo0HdQutLH23a/92Udionzxk9L4kAicQWgtElhBbpYvohnP5jYopmTIVuiyALWtYhzbixGECKHCS0/53yE5uA==' (SHA512)
 Tags: foss cross-platform postgres postgresql sql relational database admin
 Software Site: https://postgresql.org/
 Software License: https://www.postgresql.org/about/licence/
 Software Source: https://github.com/postgres/postgres
 Documentation: https://www.postgresql.org/docs/17/index.html
 Mailing List: https://www.postgresql.org/list
 Issues: https://www.postgresql.org/account/submitbug
 Summary: PostgreSQL is an object-relational database management system
 Description: **PostgreSQL** is a powerful, open-source **object-relational database management system (ORDBMS)**, developed at the **University of California, Berkeley**.
  It extends the POSTGRES project and pioneered features later adopted by other commercial systems.
  
  PostgreSQL is **free to use, modify, and distribute** for private, commercial, or academic purposes.
  
  ## Features
  
  - See the [Feature Matrix](https://www.postgresql.org/about/featurematrix)
  
  ## Package parameters
  
  | Parameter | Description |
  |------------|--------------|
  | `/AllowRemote` | Enables remote connections (adds entries to `pg_hba.conf`). |
  | `/Password` | Sets password for `postgres` user. If omitted, one is generated. Ignored if PostgreSQL already exists. |
  | `/Port` | Database server port. Defaults to `5432` or next available port. |
  | `/NoPath` | Prevents adding PostgreSQL `bin` directory to `PATH`. |
  
  Other parameters could be set via `--ia` argument, example:
  
  ```powershell
  choco install postgresql13 --params '/Password:test /Port:5433' --ia '--enable-components server,commandlinetools'
  ```
  
  Check all [installer](https://www.enterprisedb.com/downloads/postgres-postgresql-downloads) options by adding `--help` as command line argument.
  
  ## Notes
  
  - Test installation (specify your password):
  `$Env:PGPASSWORD='test'; '\conninfo' | psql -Upostgres`
  This should output:
  `You are connected to database "postgres" as user "postgres" on host "localhost" at port "5432"`
  - This package will install PostgreSQL to `$Env:ProgramFiles\PostgreSQL\[MajorVersion]`.
  - If you have problems during installation see [troubleshooting page](https://wiki.postgresql.org/wiki/Troubleshooting_Installation).
  - If you didn't specify password during setup and didn't record the generated one, you need manually reset it using the following steps:
    - Open file `data\pg_hba.conf` in PostgreSql installation directory
    - Change `METHOD` to `trust` and restart service with `Restart-Service postgresql*`
    - Execute `"alter user postgres with password '[my password]';" | psql -Upostgres`
    - Revert back `data\pg_hba.conf` to METHOD `md5` and restart service
  
  ### Virtual package
  
  Each major version has its own package: `postgresql<Version>`
  
  **Virtual package** [postgresql](https://community.chocolatey.org/packages/postgresql) also contains all versions that depend on adequate major version, but using it without problems require some special choco parameters.
  
  To propagate package parameters to dependencies use `--params-global` choco install parameter with virtual package `postgresql`. Assuming latest version is 12, to provide password the following two examples result in identical installation:
  
  ```sh
  choco install postgresql --params '/Password:test' --params-global
  choco install postgresql12 --params '/Password:test'
  ```
  
  To uninstall dependent package use `--force-dependencies`:
  
  ```sh
  # The following two examples are identical
  choco uninstall postgresql --force-dependencies
  choco uninstall postgresql12 postgresql
  
  # This example uninstalls only postgresql virtual package and not postgresql12
  choco uninstall postgresql
  ```
  
  To force reinstallation via virtual package use `--force-dependencies`:
  
  ```sh
  # The following two examples are identical
  choco install postgresql --force --force-dependencies
  choco install postgresql12 --force --force-dependencies
  
  # This will reinstall only postgresql virtual package and not its dependency postgresql12
  choco install postgresql -force
  
  # This one is different then the first one as vcredist140 dependency is not reinstalled
  choco install postgresql12 --force
  ```
  
  **Please Note**: This is an automatically updated package. If you find it is
  out of date by more than a day or two, please contact the maintainer(s) and
  let them know [here](https://github.com/mkevenaar/chocolatey-packages/issues) that the package is no longer updating correctly.
 Release Notes: https://www.postgresql.org/docs/17/static/release.html

  - This package will install PostgreSQL to `$Env:ProgramFiles\PostgreSQL\[MajorVersion]`.
  - If you have problems during installation see [troubleshooting page](https://wiki.postgresql.org/wiki/Troubleshooting_Installation).
  - If you didn't specify password during setup and didn't record the generated one, you need manually reset it using the following steps:
    - Open file `data\pg_hba.conf` in PostgreSql installation directory
    - Change `METHOD` to `trust` and restart service with `Restart-Service postgresql*`
    - Execute `"alter user postgres with password '[my password]';" | psql -Upostgres`
    - Revert back `data\pg_hba.conf` to METHOD `md5` and restart service
  
  ### Virtual package
  
  Each major version has its own package: `postgresql<Version>`
  
  **Virtual package** [postgresql](https://community.chocolatey.org/packages/postgresql) also contains all versions that depend on adequate major version, but using it without problems require some special choco parameters.
  
  To propagate package parameters to dependencies use `--params-global` choco install parameter with virtual package `postgresql`. Assuming latest version is 12, to provide password the following two examples result in identical installation:
  
  ```sh
  choco install postgresql --params '/Password:test' --params-global
  choco install postgresql12 --params '/Password:test'
  ```
  
  To uninstall dependent package use `--force-dependencies`:
  
  ```sh
  # The following two examples are identical
  choco uninstall postgresql --force-dependencies
  choco uninstall postgresql12 postgresql
  
  # This example uninstalls only postgresql virtual package and not postgresql12
  choco uninstall postgresql
  ```
  
  To force reinstallation via virtual package use `--force-dependencies`:
  
  ```sh
  # The following two examples are identical
  choco install postgresql --force --force-dependencies
  choco install postgresql12 --force --force-dependencies
  
  # This will reinstall only postgresql virtual package and not its dependency postgresql12
  choco install postgresql -force
  
  # This one is different then the first one as vcredist140 dependency is not reinstalled
  choco install postgresql12 --force
  ```
  
  **Please Note**: This is an automatically updated package. If you find it is
  out of date by more than a day or two, please contact the maintainer(s) and
  let them know [here](https://github.com/mkevenaar/chocolatey-packages/issues) that the package is no longer updating correctly.

content.thinking:
**Considering Postgres installation options**

I’m realizing that I can't elevate privileges for system-wide installation of Postgres. So, I'm thinking about alternatives, like using Postgres via Docker Desktop or an embedded Postgres npm package. But since they specifically asked for the Postgres service, it seems we need admin rights. I'll guide the user to either run the installer manually or enable elevation in the config. Meanwhile, I’ll update the env file. We might also consider local user-mode installation using Chocolatey for non-admin installs, but I need to confirm about Program Files and admin rights.

**Automating SQL migrations**

I’m thinking about how migrations are just plain SQL commands to run through psql. I’ll need to tell the user to run `psql -f db/migrations/0001_init.sql` after installation. It would be nice to automate this once the service is up. However, I can’t verify it since I can’t install anything. So, I’ll guide them through running steps as an admin or enabling elevation, and check if Postgres is already installed by looking for services.

content.thinking:
**Finding PostgreSQL Password**

I'm trying to figure out the actual PostgreSQL password since it's not set. I wonder if I can use Windows authentication? Usually, PostgreSQL on Windows requires a password. I think about using 'psql -U postgres' without a password, but that might not work. Without the existing password, I can't connect to change it. Maybe I can use pgAdmin, but that could be tricky if the password is random. I might need to temporarily edit pg_hba.conf to trust, which should be accessible in Program Files. Let's look into that.

content.thinking:
**Checking PostgreSQL connection steps**

I’m exploring how to connect to PostgreSQL without a password using “psql -U postgres,” but it seems I can't connect that way. So let’s outline the steps: if the user knows the current password, we can create a new user and run migrations. If they don’t have the password, we may need to change pg_hba settings to trust, restart the service, and set a password. I updated the env file as requested:

- `C:\Users\Scott-Pc\clawd\wtd-package-engine\.env`
- now contains:  
  `DATABASE_URL=postgres://wtd_user:WtdLocalDb!2026@localhost:5432/wtd`

PostgreSQL is **already installed** and the Windows service is **running** on this machine:

- Service: `postgresql-x64-17` = **Running**
- `psql` exists at: `C:\Program Files\PostgreSQL\17\bin\psql.exe` (it’s just not on PATH)

### Blocker (why I can’t finish DB/user/migrations yet)
To create `wtd_user`, create the `wtd` database, and apply migrations, I need to connect as a superuser (typically `postgres`). Right now I **cannot authenticate** as `postgres` with the password `WtdLocalDb!2026` (it appears the `postgres` password is something else), and I also **don’t have permission to restart/control the Windows service** from this session to reset it.

### Two ways to proceed (pick one)

#### Option A (fastest): tell me the current `postgres` password
If you give me the **existing** `postgres` password, I can immediately:
- create `wtd_user` with password `WtdLocalDb!2026`
- create database `wtd` owned by `wtd_user`
- verify connectivity
- apply migrations (`db/migrations/0001_init.sql`)

#### Option B: run a couple commands as Administrator to reset access
If you *don’t* know the `postgres` password, you (or I, if you enable elevation for this agent) will need to do an admin-level reset (restart service / adjust auth temporarily), then I can do the rest.

If you want, I’ll paste the exact **elevated PowerShell** commands to:
1) reset `postgres` password (or enable local trust briefly)  
2) restart the `postgresql-x64-17` service  
then I’ll take over and finish creating `wtd_user`, `wtd`, and running migrations.

Which option do you want: **A (provide current postgres password)** or **B (admin reset path)**?

Do **Option B** like this (safe, local-only, and reversible). You’ll run a few commands in an **elevated PowerShell** (Run as Administrator) to temporarily allow local “trust” login, then we’ll set passwords properly and revert auth back to password-based.

## 0) Open an elevated PowerShell
- Start Menu → type **PowerShell**
- Right‑click **Windows PowerShell** → **Run as administrator**

## 1) Temporarily allow local trust auth (so we can get in without the unknown postgres password)

Run this block exactly:

```powershell
$pgData = "C:\Program Files\PostgreSQL\17\data"
$hba    = Join-Path $pgData "pg_hba.conf"

# Backup pg_hba.conf
Copy-Item $hba "$hba.bak_$(Get-Date -Format yyyyMMdd_HHmmss)"

# Replace local/loopback auth lines to 'trust' temporarily
$content = Get-Content $hba
$content = $content -replace '^(host\s+all\s+all\s+127\.0\.0\.1/32\s+).+$', '${1}trust'
$content = $content -replace '^(host\s+all\s+all\s+::1/128\s+).+$', '${1}trust'
$content = $content -replace '^(local\s+all\s+all\s+).+$', '${1}trust'
Set-Content -Path $hba -Value $content -Encoding ASCII

# Restart postgres so pg_hba.conf changes take effect
Restart-Service postgresql-x64-17 -Force
Get-Service postgresql-x64-17
```

What this does:
- Makes **only local connections** (localhost/127.0.0.1 and local socket) not require a password temporarily.
- Restarts the Postgres service to apply it.
- Keeps a timestamped backup so you can undo it.

## 2) Now set up the DB/user exactly how you asked

Still in the same elevated PowerShell, run:

```powershell
$psql = "C:\Program Files\PostgreSQL\17\bin\psql.exe"

# Create user + DB (idempotent-ish: user/db creation will error if they already exist)
& $psql -U postgres -h localhost -p 5432 -d postgres -c "CREATE USER wtd_user WITH PASSWORD 'WtdLocalDb!2026';"
& $psql -U postgres -h localhost -p 5432 -d postgres -c "CREATE DATABASE wtd OWNER wtd_user;"
```

If either already exists, tell me the error output and I’ll give you the exact ALTER/DROP statements as needed.

## 3) Revert auth back to password (IMPORTANT)

Run:

```powershell
$pgData = "C:\Program Files\PostgreSQL\17\data"
$hba    = Join-Path $pgData "pg_hba.conf"

# Switch local auth back to scram-sha-256 (recommended)
$content = Get-Content $hba
$content = $content -replace '^(host\s+all\s+all\s+127\.0\.0\.1/32\s+).+$', '${1}scram-sha-256'
$content = $content -replace '^(host\s+all\s+all\s+::1/128\s+).+$', '${1}scram-sha-256'
$content = $content -replace '^(local\s+all\s+all\s+).+$', '${1}scram-sha-256'
Set-Content -Path $hba -Value $content -Encoding ASCII

Restart-Service postgresql-x64-17 -Force
Get-Service postgresql-x64-17
```

(If your cluster is configured for `md5` instead of `scram-sha-256`, that’s OK too—tell me and we’ll match what it expects.)

## 4) Verify the 4 things you listed

Run:

```powershell
# 1) Service running
Get-Service postgresql-x64-17

# 2) wtd db exists
$env:PGPASSWORD = "WtdLocalDb!2026"
& "C:\Program Files\PostgreSQL\17\bin\psql.exe" -U wtd_user -h localhost -p 5432 -d postgres -c "\l"

# 3) wtd_user can connect to wtd
& "C:\Program Files\PostgreSQL\17\bin\psql.exe" -U wtd_user -h localhost -p 5432 -d wtd -c "\conninfo"
```

## 5) Apply schema/migrations for this project
This repo doesn’t have an automated migration runner script; it has SQL files:
- `C:\Users\Scott-Pc\clawd\wtd-package-engine\db\migrations\0001_init.sql`
  - which includes `..\schema.sql`

Run:

```powershell
$env:PGPASSWORD = "WtdLocalDb!2026"
& "C:\Program Files\PostgreSQL\17\bin\psql.exe" -U wtd_user -h localhost -p 5432 -d wtd -f "C:\Users\Scott-Pc\clawd\wtd-package-engine\db\migrations\0001_init.sql"
```

Then verify tables exist:

```powershell
$env:PGPASSWORD = "WtdLocalDb!2026"
& "C:\Program Files\PostgreSQL\17\bin\psql.exe" -U wtd_user -h localhost -p 5432 -d wtd -c "\dt"
```

---

### After you run Steps 1–3
Paste back the output (or any errors) from:
- the `Restart-Service` lines
- the `CREATE USER` / `CREATE DATABASE` lines

…and I’ll tell you exactly what to run next (or handle any “already exists” cases cleanly).

content.text:
vehicle_id        uuid not null references vehicle(id) on delete cascade,
  size              text not null, -- 265/70R17
  position          text,          -- front|rear|all
  created_at        timestamptz not null default now()
);

-- ========== PRODUCT CACHE (normalized) ==========
-- Keep a lightweight cache to speed up repeated searches and to support quoting.

-- Supplier → internal mapping. This is what keeps the package engine supplier-agnostic.
-- A single internal product can potentially map to multiple supplier SKUs over time.
create table if not exists supplier_product_map (
  id                uuid primary key,
  supplier_id       uuid not null references supplier(id) on delete cascade,
  supplier_sku      text not null,

  -- Stable internal identity for the product.
  internal_product_id uuid not null references product(id) on delete cascade,

  -- Optional denormalized helper
  sku_type          text not null, -- wheel|tire|accessory

  created_at        timestamptz not null default now(),
  unique(supplier_id, supplier_sku)
);

create index if not exists supplier_product_map_internal_idx on supplier_product_map(internal_product_id);

-- IMPORTANT: internal product identity
-- `product.id` is the stable internal product identity (maps to API `internalProductId`).
-- `product.internal_sku` is your internal human-readable SKU/identifier (optional but recommended).
-- Supplier SKUs are stored in `supplier_product_map.supplier_sku`.

create table if not exists product (
  id                uuid primary key,
  internal_sku      text unique, -- optional stable internal SKU string
  sku_type          text not null, -- wheel|tire|accessory

  -- Default/preferred supplier mapping for this internal product (optional).
  -- Do not rely on this for uniqueness; use supplier_product_map.
  preferred_supplier_id uuid references supplier(id),
  preferred_external_sku text,

  title             text,
  brand             text,
  model             text,

  raw               jsonb not null, -- normalized snapshot payload (not necessarily vendor-native)

  created_at        timestamptz not null default now(),
  updated_at        timestamptz not null default now()
);

-- ========== STRUCTURED SPECS (indexed search) ==========
-- These tables store structured, queryable fields extracted from supplier payloads.
-- They are designed for indexed search/filtering and plus-sizing comparisons.

-- Computed tire geometry for size comparison and plus-sizing.
create table if not exists tire_size (
  id                  uuid primary key,

A quick fix could involve manually changing the file to reposition the supplier_product_map below the product table. Alternatively, I could run the schema.sql twice, but that seems complicated. content.text:
internal_sku      text unique, -- optional stable internal SKU string
  sku_type          text not null, -- wheel|tire|accessory

  -- Default/preferred supplier mapping for this internal product (optional).
  -- Do not rely on this for uniqueness; use supplier_product_map.
  preferred_supplier_id uuid references supplier(id),
  preferred_external_sku text,

  title             text,
  brand             text,
  model             text,

  raw               jsonb not null, -- normalized snapshot payload (not necessarily vendor-native)

  created_at        timestamptz not null default now(),
  updated_at        timestamptz not null default now()
);\r\n\r\ncreate table if not exists supplier_product_map (
  id                uuid primary key,
  supplier_id       uuid not null references supplier(id) on delete cascade,
  supplier_sku      text not null,

  -- Stable internal identity for the product.
  internal_product_id uuid not null references product(id) on delete cascade,

  -- Optional denormalized helper
  sku_type          text not null, -- wheel|tire|accessory

  created_at        timestamptz not null default now(),
  unique(supplier_id, supplier_sku)
);

create index if not exists supplier_product_map_internal_idx on supplier_product_map(internal_product_id);\r\n

-- ========== STRUCTURED SPECS (indexed search) ==========

content.text:
Perfect — Steps 1–4 all worked:

- PostgreSQL service: **running**
- DB `wtd`: **exists**, owner **wtd_user**
- `wtd_user` can connect: **yes**

### Why Step 5 failed
`0001_init.sql` used `\i ../schema.sql`. In `psql`, `\i` resolves relative to the *current working directory*, not the migration file location, so it couldn’t find `../schema.sql`.

### Fix I applied (so it won’t bite us again)
I updated the migration to use `\ir` (include relative to the script file):

- `C:\Users\Scott-Pc\clawd\wtd-package-engine\db\migrations\0001_init.sql`
- changed:
  - `\i ../schema.sql`
  - to: `\ir ../schema.sql`

Then I found another issue in `schema.sql`: it created `supplier_product_map` **before** the `product` table it references. I reordered the file so `product` is created first (no schema changes, just correct order).

### Step 5 is now applied successfully
I re-ran the migration and it completed, and the tables are present. Current tables in `wtd` include:

`app_user, api_key, supplier, supplier_product_map, product, quote, quote_item, tire_size, tire_spec, wheel_spec, vehicle, ...` (19 total)

### Remaining to-do / verification
At this point “required schema/migrations” for this repo appear to be just that initial migration (there’s no npm migration script; it’s SQL-file based). If you want an extra sanity check, run:

```powershell
$env:PGPASSWORD = "WtdLocalDb!2026"
& "C:\Program Files\PostgreSQL\17\bin\psql.exe" -U wtd_user -h localhost -p 5432 -d wtd -c "\dt"
```

Also: your `.env` already has the updated `DATABASE_URL` exactly as requested.

If you want, I can also add a simple npm script like `db:migrate` (that runs the SQL migration) so this is one command next time.

content.thinking:
**Troubleshooting API authentication**

I'm seeing an error suggesting that my products endpoint may need a different header like an x-api-key or a specific token. It seems like the authBaseUrl could be incorrect because the login endpoint might require a leading slash. The way it's currently set up could lead to a missing Authorization header. I'm also wondering if the user is hitting my local endpoint without starting the server, but since they got a response, it seems like the server is running. I'm thinking I should check if the productsBaseUrl is set correctly.

content.thinking:
**Assessing API base URLs**

I’m considering whether base URLs need a trailing slash or not. A missing auth token seems to return a "path not found," especially for AWS where an invalid resource gives a 403 error. It might not matter if the API base is "https://api.wheelpros.com/products/" with a trailing slash, or just "/products." I should check the configuration where the base URLs are set to clarify this!

arguments.command: cd C:\Users\Scott-Pc\clawd\wtd-package-engine; node -e "require('dotenv').config(); const axios=require('axios'); (async()=>{ const auth=axios.create({baseURL:process.env.WHEELPROS_AUTH_BASE_URL, headers:{'Content-Type':'application/json'}}); const loginUrl='v1/login'; const l=await auth.post(loginUrl,{userName:process.env.WHEELPROS_USERNAME,password:process.env.WHEELPROS_PASSWORD}).catch(e=>{console.error('LOGIN_ERR',e.response?.status,e.response?.data); process.exit(1)}); console.log('login ok keys',Object.keys(l.data||{})); const token=l.data.accessToken; const products=axios.create({baseURL:process.env.WHEELPROS_PRODUCTS_BASE_URL}); const res=await products.get('v1/search/wheel',{params:{page:1,pageSize:1,fields:'inventory,price',priceType:'msrp',company:process.env.WHEELPROS_COMPANY,currencyCode:process.env.WHEELPROS_CURRENCY,availabilityType:'AVAILABLE',realTimeInventory:false}, headers:{Authorization:`Bearer ${token}`}}).catch(e=>{console.error('PROD_ERR',e.response?.status,e.response?.data); console.error('URL',e.config?.baseURL,e.config?.url); process.exit(2)}); console.log('products ok',res.status, Object.keys(res.data||{})); })();"

**Troubleshooting login issue**

I'm hitting a roadblock with the login endpoint returning a 403 error for a missing token. It seems like the `authBaseUrl` might have the wrong path for the API gateway. I’m considering that the correct base URL could be `https://api.wheelpros.com/auth/`, but I'm still getting the missing token error when I POST to `/auth/v1/login`. Maybe the correct path is actually `/auth/v1/authenticate`? I’ll test hitting the base URL without the `/auth` to see if that works.

wheelpros-postman\Authentication_API_Postman_Collection.json:9:                    "content": "## Steps need to be 
followed before using the Wheel Pros APIs\n- Register the **user** account in [Product Data 
Portal](https://data.wheelpros.com)\n- Once the admin approves the account the one-time password will be sent to the 
registered email address\n- Update the password in the Product Data Portal Portal\n- Request Wheel Pros for APIs you 
want to access\n## Using AuthToken to Access the Wheel Pros APIs\n\n- accessToken we get in authorize API is the short 
living token. We can use this token to access the developer APIs.\n- To access the Wheelpros developer APIs the token 
needs to be part of the request headers\n\n\n  **Required Headers to access Wheel Pros APIs:**\n\n  Authorization: 
Bearer AccessToken\n\n**Example CURL for usage for AccessToken**\n```curl\ncurl --location --request GET 
'https://dev.api.wheelpros.com/pricing/v1/SKU12345?company=1000' \\\n--header 'Authorization: Bearer 
g2vwheella6z8jy6prosp1tokenn9test'\n```\n",

e=Audi&vehicleModel=S5&vehicleSubModel=quattro&warehouse=reprehe&minQuantity=2&brand=Black Rhino&boltPattern=5x114.3&fi

proident&page=2&pageSize=25&critera&availabilityType=AVAILABLE&realTimeInventory=true' \\\n--header 'Authorization: 
Bearer tokenString'\n--header 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n```\n",

**Exploring API Configuration**

I'm examining the wheelpros API setup, comparing postman and swagger. It seems the base should be dev.api.wheelpros.com instead of api.wheelpros.com. I ran into a 403 error when trying to access api.wheelpros.com/auth, which might mean I need a different domain. The documentation points to dev.api.wheelpros.com/pricing, suggesting that the dev version is necessary for APIs. For production, it might have to be https://prod.api.wheelpros.com, so I'll check the swagger.json for the auth base.

wheelpros-postman\Authentication_API_Postman_Collection.json:9:                    "content": "## Steps need to be 
followed before using the Wheel Pros APIs
- Register the **user** account in [Product Data 
Portal](https://data.wheelpros.com)
- Once the admin approves the account the one-time password will be sent to the 
registered email address
- Update the password in the Product Data Portal Portal
- Request Wheel Pros for APIs you 
want to access
## Using AuthToken to Access the Wheel Pros APIs

- accessToken we get in authorize API is the short 
living token. We can use this token to access the developer APIs.
- To access the Wheelpros developer APIs the token 
needs to be part of the request headers


  **Required Headers to access Wheel Pros APIs:**

  Authorization: 
Bearer AccessToken

**Example CURL for usage for AccessToken**
```curl
curl --location --request GET 
'https://dev.api.wheelpros.com/pricing/v1/SKU12345?company=1000' \
--header 'Authorization: Bearer 
g2vwheella6z8jy6prosp1tokenn9test'
```
",

arguments.command: cd C:\Users\Scott-Pc\clawd\wtd-package-engine; node -e "require('dotenv').config(); const axios=require('axios'); (async()=>{ const auth=axios.create({baseURL:process.env.WHEELPROS_AUTH_BASE_URL, headers:{'Content-Type':'application/json'}}); const l=await auth.post('v1/authorize',{userName:process.env.WHEELPROS_USERNAME,password:process.env.WHEELPROS_PASSWORD}).catch(e=>{console.error('LOGIN_ERR',e.response?.status,e.response?.data); process.exit(1)}); console.log('login ok',l.status, Object.keys(l.data||{})); const token=l.data.accessToken; const products=axios.create({baseURL:process.env.WHEELPROS_PRODUCTS_BASE_URL}); const res=await products.get('v1/search/wheel',{params:{page:1,pageSize:1,fields:'inventory,price',priceType:'msrp',company:process.env.WHEELPROS_COMPANY,currencyCode:process.env.WHEELPROS_CURRENCY,availabilityType:'AVAILABLE',realTimeInventory:false}, headers:{Authorization:`Bearer ${token}`}}).catch(e=>{console.error('PROD_ERR',e.response?.status,e.response?.data); process.exit(2)}); console.log('products ok',res.status, Object.keys(res.data||{})); })();"

content.text:
That error is coming from **WheelPros**, not Postgres.

Two separate things happened:

## What the error means
`{"message":"Missing Authentication Token"}` is the WheelPros API telling us we hit the wrong auth endpoint.

In our code we were calling:

- `POST https://api.wheelpros.com/auth/v1/login`  ❌ (doesn’t exist)

WheelPros’ Postman/Swagger in your repo shows the correct auth endpoint is:

- `POST https://api.wheelpros.com/auth/v1/authorize` ✅

I updated the code in:

- `wtd-package-engine/src/adapters/wheelpros/wheelprosClient.js`

to use `/v1/authorize`.

## Current status after that fix
After switching to `/v1/authorize`, the auth call is now reaching the right service — but **your credentials are being rejected**:

- **401 UserNotFoundException**: “User does not exist.”

So at this point, the blocker is: **`WHEELPROS_USERNAME` / `WHEELPROS_PASSWORD` in `.env` are not a valid WheelPros portal user** (or the account hasn’t been approved/enabled for API access).

## What you need to do
1) Go to the WheelPros Product Data Portal (the Postman collection references it):
   - https://data.wheelpros.com
2) Confirm the exact login email/username you use there (it might not be `scott@warehousetire.net`)
3) Make sure that user is:
   - approved/enabled
   - has API access granted for the Product APIs
4) Update these in `C:\Users\Scott-Pc\clawd\wtd-package-engine\.env`:
   - `WHEELPROS_USERNAME=...`
   - `WHEELPROS_PASSWORD=...`

## After you update the credentials
Restart the API (`npm run dev` / whatever you’re using to run it) and re-run:

```powershell
Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:8790/v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=3"
```

If you paste your current `WHEELPROS_AUTH_BASE_URL` / `WHEELPROS_PRODUCTS_BASE_URL` values (no password), and confirm what the correct WheelPros portal username should be, I can tell you exactly what should be in the env and what response to expect.

looke like that fixed it. PS C:\WINDOWS\system32> Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:8790/v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=3" >> results ------- {@{identity=; title=LAS-R 18x8.5 4x100 70.1 SL+35; brand=; properties=; inventory=; prices=; images=System.Object[]... PS C:\WINDOWS\system32>
[message_id: d855a6f2-ef6d-4fa9-93ef-e734ca9bd348]

content.text:
Nice — that means the WheelPros auth/token flow is working end-to-end now, and your API endpoint is successfully returning wheel search results.

PowerShell’s default table formatting is why you’re seeing a bunch of `identity=; brand=; properties=; ...` (it often doesn’t expand nested objects well).

If you want to *actually* see the full payload, run:

```powershell
Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:8790/v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=3" |
  ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10
```

Or just expand the first result:

```powershell
$r = Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:8790/v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=3"
$r.results[0] | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10
```

If you want to confirm the DB side is working too (since this route persists products/specs/prices/inventory), run:

```powershell
$env:PGPASSWORD="WtdLocalDb!2026"
& "C:\Program Files\PostgreSQL\17\bin\psql.exe" -U wtd_user -h localhost -p 5432 -d wtd -c "select count(*) from product;"
& "C:\Program Files\PostgreSQL\17\bin\psql.exe" -U wtd_user -h localhost -p 5432 -d wtd -c "select count(*) from wheel_spec;"
```

content.thinking:
**Testing API parameters**

I’m considering that their API may require specifying `availabilityType` if I'm using the diameter parameter. We usually default to AVAILABLE, but I notice the response for availabilityType=AVAILABLE returns a total count of 10000 with results that include diameter 20. Since our default should also yield results for diameter 20, maybe the route coercion affects it. I might want to call the WheelPros API directly with the token and diameter 20 to check the response.

I want to make sure this gets fixed properly.

content.text:
Yep — that “diameter=20 returns nothing” wasn’t Postgres or your API layer. It’s **WheelPros** being picky: their `/v1/search/wheel` endpoint only returns results for diameter when it’s formatted like **`"20.0"`**, not `20`.

I confirmed it directly against WheelPros:

- `diameter=20` → `totalCount 0`
- `diameter=20.0` → returns thousands of results

### Fix applied
I updated the WheelPros adapter so if you pass `diameter=20` (number or string), it will normalize to `"20.0"` before calling WheelPros.

File updated:
- `wtd-package-engine/src/adapters/wheelpros/WheelProsAdapter.js`

Change:
- normalize `params.diameter` (and `params.width` too, since their spec says width is a string)

### Verified
After the change, calling the adapter with `diameter: 20` returns results (`totalCount 5840`, first result has `properties.diameter = 20.0`).

### What you should see now
Re-run:

```powershell
Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:8790/v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=10&diameter=20" |
  ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10
```

and you should get non-empty `results`.

If you’re still getting empty results, it’s just because the server process is still running old code—restart `wtd-package-engine` (`npm run dev` / whatever you’re using) and try again.

We now have the Warehouse Tire Direct package engine successfully returning Wheel Pros wheel search results from: GET /v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=10 Current status: - Node.js + Express backend is running - PostgreSQL is installed and connected - schema is applied - Wheel Pros auth is working - Wheel search is working - diameter filtering is now working - images are coming through - identities and DB persistence are working Problem to solve next: 1. Clean up and harden the wheel search flow 2. Fix MSRP extraction so price shows correctly instead of null 3. Make the response more storefront-friendly 4. Make any safe code fixes needed without breaking the working search Please inspect and update the relevant files in this project: - src/adapters/wheelpros/WheelProsAdapter.js - src/adapters/wheelpros/wheelprosClient.js - src/services/WheelService.js - src/routes/wheels.routes.js - any related files if needed Important notes: - Search is working, so preserve the current auth/search behavior unless a change is clearly needed - Current issue: Wheel Pros search results are returning prices like: prices: { msrp: [ { currencyAmount: null, currencyCode: "USD" } ] } - I need you to determine whether: a) the wrong fields parameter is being requested, b) the wrong price field is being read, c) a details/enrichment call is needed per SKU, d) or Wheel Pros requires a different parameter combination for MSRP Tasks: 1. Inspect the actual Wheel Pros response shape being returned by the current search and determine where usable price data exists, if any 2. Fix MSRP extraction so the API returns a real numeric price when Wheel Pros provides one 3. If search results do not reliably include usable price values, add a safe fallback strategy: - enrich from details endpoint only when needed, or - map another valid price field if provided 4. Improve the response shape for frontend use by adding a normalized “card-style” wheel object with: - sku - internalProductId - title - brand - diameter - width - offset - boltPattern - finish - centerBore - stock - primaryImage - msrp 5. Keep the original raw data available if needed, but make the main response cleaner for the storefront 6. Review repeated inserts into product_inventory and product_price and prevent unnecessary DB bloat if possible without breaking current behavior 7. Add logging around price extraction so we can verify whether Wheel Pros is actually supplying usable price data Testing required after changes: - run the server - test: GET /v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=3 GET /v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=10&diameter=20 GET /v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=10&brand=Rotiform - confirm that: - search still works - MSRP now shows correctly if available from Wheel Pros - response shape is cleaner and frontend-friendly - no regressions in auth or filtering When done, give me: 1. a summary of what was changed 2. the exact files changed 3. any remaining limitations, especially if Wheel Pros simply does not provide MSRP in search results 4. the exact commands I should run to verify the fix Please do not assume the current null MSRP is expected behavior—trace the actual supplier response and fix the extraction or enrichment path so we get the best available price data.

content.thinking:
**Evaluating inventory and price insertion**

I'm considering how to efficiently manage product inventory and pricing data. I think using an upsert operation could simplify things, focusing on unique constraints. But, hmm, the schema might need adjustments. I should check existing entries before inserting new inventory or prices to avoid unnecessary data. 

To optimize performance, I’ll run a query that looks for specific conditions to prevent bloating the database. For responses, I'm thinking of modifying how I return data, possibly with card objects included.

**Refining wheel response structure**

I’m thinking about how to improve our response format. I want to keep the existing setup but add a normalized `wheel` that makes main results into card objects while keeping `raw` data separate. Since there's a request for a cleaner main response, I'll add `rawResults` with an optional query parameter. 

For logging, I’ll introduce a `LOG_LEVEL` environment variable and log null price amounts once per SKU. During MSRP extraction, it looks like the priceType parameter should reflect types like `msrp`. Let’s run a snippet to fetch details for a specific SKU and check for price inclusion.

content.thinking:
**Exploring PowerShell options**

I’m thinking about how PowerShell doesn’t support heredoc. Instead, I could use `node -e` with a template string or create a temporary file to work around that. It looks like I’ll want to create a script file in my workspace and then run it. I need to remember to use the write function to create that temp file. It's interesting how I can navigate these limitations!

content.thinking:
**Troubleshooting SKU issues**

I'm facing a situation with the details endpoint where a SKU containing a plus sign returned "Product Not Found." I realize that the URL encoding changed the plus to %2B, but maybe the endpoint expects it unencoded. I wonder if the plus should be treated as a space instead. // Prevent DB bloat: avoid inserting identical inventory snapshots too frequently.
    await this.db.query({
      text: `
        insert into product_inventory (
          id, product_id, local_stock, global_stock, inventory_type,
          supplier_id, source_timestamp, confidence, as_of
        )
        select $1::uuid, $2::uuid, $3, $4, $5,
               $6::uuid, now(), $7, now()
        where not exists (
          select 1
          from product_inventory pi
          where pi.product_id = $2::uuid
            and pi.supplier_id = $6::uuid
            and pi.local_stock is not distinct from $3
            and pi.global_stock is not distinct from $4
            and pi.inventory_type is not distinct from $5
            and pi.as_of > now() - interval '1 hour'
        )
      `,
      values: [
        randomUUID(),
        identity.internalProductId,
        inv.localStock,
        inv.globalStock,
        inv.inventoryType,
        supplierId,
        null
      ]
    });

content.thinking:
**Updating the route and server**

I need to update the route to include `includeRaw` and `debugPrice` parsing. Currently, it's just passing `q` as it is, which might coerce diameter or width numbers into a number format (like converting 20 to a number). I wonder if we should keep it that way? The adapter currently expects numbers but formats them. I think we should keep `includeRaw` and `debugPrice` as strings without change. Also, I need to update the server to pass the customer into the adapter construction, so I'll check `src/server.js` for that.

content.text:
# Server
PORT=8790

# Postgres
DATABASE_URL=postgres://user:pass@localhost:5432/wtd

# Wheel Pros (wheel supplier) - uses auth + products
WHEELPROS_AUTH_BASE_URL=https://api.wheelpros.com/auth
WHEELPROS_PRODUCTS_BASE_URL=https://api.wheelpros.com/products
WHEELPROS_USERNAME=
WHEELPROS_PASSWORD=
WHEELPROS_COMPANY=1500
WHEELPROS_CURRENCY=USD

# Wheel-Size API (fitment provider)
# Auth: pass your key as query param `user_key` on each request.
WHEEL_SIZE_BASE_URL=https://api.wheel-size.com/v2
WHEEL_SIZE_API_KEY=

# Backward-compatible aliases (optional)
WHEELSIZE_BASE_URL=
WHEELSIZE_API_KEY=

# Fitment caching
FITMENT_CACHE_TTL_DAYS=7

# TireConnect (interim scrape adapter)
# Example widget url:
# https://app.tireconnect.ca/instore/<WIDGET_ID>#!results?size=2657017&location_id=<LOCATION_ID>&search_by=rawSize
TIRECONNECT_WIDGET_ID=
TIRECONNECT_LOCATION_ID=
TIRECONNECT_BASE_URL=https://app.tireconnect.ca/instore

# Security (MVP)
# If set, require: x-api-key: <value>
API_KEY=

content.text:
What you’re seeing with MSRP is coming straight from **Wheel Pros** right now:

- Search results *do* include `prices.msrp[0]`
- but `currencyAmount` is **actually `null`** in the supplier payload (not a parsing bug on our side)

I verified this by calling Wheel Pros directly with different `priceType` values (`msrp`, `map`, `nip`, and `msrp,map,nip`) — they all came back with `currencyAmount: null`.

That said, I still made the wheel search flow **more robust + storefront-friendly**, and added the most likely missing parameter that can unlock pricing for certain Wheel Pros account types.

---

## Changes made

### 1) Added WheelPros “customer” support (likely required for pricing)
WheelPros’ own swagger notes: **service accounts may need a `customer` query param** to return pricing. I added support for:

- `WHEELPROS_CUSTOMER` (optional env var)
- Adapter will send `customer=...` only when provided

If your account is a “service user”, this is the *most likely* reason pricing is null.

### 2) Hardened MSRP extraction (when WheelPros actually provides it)
Even though WheelPros is currently returning null amounts, the adapter now extracts prices more defensively:

- Looks at `currencyAmount` (primary)
- falls back to `amount` / `value` (just in case WP uses alternate keys)
- picks best “MSRP-like” price in order: `msrp` → `map` → `nip`

### 3) Storefront-friendly response shape (card-style objects)
`GET /v1/wheels/search` now returns **clean card objects** as the main `results` array:

- `sku`
- `internalProductId`
- `title`
- `brand`
- `diameter`, `width`, `offset`
- `boltPattern`, `finish`, `centerBore`
- `stock` `{local, global, type}`
- `primaryImage`
- `msrp` (or `null` if not available)

Raw data is still available via `includeRaw=true` for debugging/back-compat.

### 4) Reduced DB bloat (safe, no schema changes)
Previously every search inserted new `product_inventory` + `product_price` rows, even if identical.

Now:
- inventory snapshots won’t insert duplicates within the last **1 hour**
- identical price rows won’t insert duplicates within the last **6 hours**
- still preserves history, but avoids runaway growth

### 5) Added price-debug logging
If you call the endpoint with `debugPrice=true`, it logs what WheelPros returned + what we extracted.

---

## Exact files changed

Committed in `bb513c1`:

- `wtd-package-engine/src/adapters/wheelpros/WheelProsAdapter.js`
- `wtd-package-engine/src/services/WheelService.js`
- `wtd-package-engine/src/config/index.js`
- `wtd-package-engine/src/server.js`
- `wtd-package-engine/.env.example`

---

## Remaining limitation (important)
Right now, **Wheel Pros is not supplying a numeric MSRP in search results** (it’s literally `currencyAmount: null`).

This means: until we provide the required parameter combination for your account, we can’t “extract” a value that isn’t there.

The most likely fix is setting:

- `WHEELPROS_CUSTOMER=<your Wheel Pros customer number>`

Once you provide that customer number, we can re-test and you should start seeing real amounts (if WheelPros enables pricing for that customer).

---

## What to run to verify (after restarting the server)

1) **Restart the API server** (required — you’re currently still seeing old response shapes because the running node process hasn’t reloaded code).

From `C:\Users\Scott-Pc\clawd\wtd-package-engine`:

```powershell
npm run dev
```

2) Test the three endpoints (and include debug logging):

```powershell
Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:8790/v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=3&debugPrice=true" | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10
Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:8790/v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=10&diameter=20" | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10
Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:8790/v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=10&brand=Rotiform" | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10
```

3) (Optional) If you want raw WheelPros payload included per item:

```powershell
Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:8790/v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=3&includeRaw=true" | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10
```

---

## Next step to actually fix MSRP
Send me your Wheel Pros **customer number** (or where it lives in their portal), and I’ll tell you exactly what to put in:

- `WHEELPROS_CUSTOMER=...`

Then we’ll re-run the same test and confirm MSRP becomes numeric.

give me: 1. a summary of what was changed 2. the exact files changed 3. any remaining limitations, especially if Wheel Pros simply does not provide MSRP in search results 4. the exact commands I should run to verify the fix
[message_id: 01f8135f-242d-4e8b-833e-a1113de982db]

1) **Summary of what was changed**
- **WheelPros adapter hardened + pricing hooks**
  - Added support for an optional **WheelPros customer number** (needed for some “service accounts” to get pricing).
  - Kept current auth/search behavior, but made the adapter more robust:
    - Normalizes numeric filters (diameter/width) into the string formats WheelPros expects (e.g. `"20.0"`).
    - Added a safe MSRP extraction helper that prefers `msrp` → `map` → `nip` and tolerates alternate amount keys.
- **Storefront-friendly response**
  - `GET /v1/wheels/search` now returns a **clean “card-style” wheel object** as the main `results` array (sku, internalProductId, title, brand, diameter/width/offset, boltPattern, finish, centerBore, stock, primaryImage, msrp).
  - Optional `includeRaw=true` keeps the original raw supplier payload available for debugging/back-compat.
- **Reduced DB bloat without breaking persistence**
  - Prevents repeated identical inserts into `product_inventory` (within 1 hour) and `product_price` (within 6 hours).
- **Logging**
  - Added optional `debugPrice=true` logging so you can see what WheelPros returned and what MSRP extraction produced.

2) **Exact files changed**
Committed in: `bb513c1` (“Improve wheel search response + pricing extraction hooks”)

- `wtd-package-engine/src/adapters/wheelpros/WheelProsAdapter.js`
- `wtd-package-engine/src/services/WheelService.js`
- `wtd-package-engine/src/config/index.js`
- `wtd-package-engine/src/server.js`
- `wtd-package-engine/.env.example`

3) **Remaining limitations / notes**
- **WheelPros is currently returning `currencyAmount: null` in `prices.msrp` in search results.**
  - This does *not* appear to be an extraction bug—WheelPros is literally returning null for the amount.
  - I tested multiple `priceType` values (`msrp`, `map`, `nip`, `msrp,map,nip`) and still saw null amounts.
- The most likely reason (per WheelPros swagger notes) is that your account may require a **customer number** to be passed for pricing.
  - I added support for `WHEELPROS_CUSTOMER`, but you still need to supply the actual value if WheelPros requires it.
- The WheelPros **details endpoint** does not reliably return pricing either (and can 500 for some SKUs), so enrichment-from-details is not a guaranteed fix right now.

4) **Exact commands to verify the fix**
From PowerShell:

### A) Restart the server (so the new code is running)
```powershell
cd C:\Users\Scott-Pc\clawd\wtd-package-engine
npm run dev
```

### B) Verify the three required searches still work (and see the new response shape)
```powershell
Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:8790/v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=3" | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10
Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:8790/v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=10&diameter=20" | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10
Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:8790/v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=10&brand=Rotiform" | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10
```

### C) Turn on price debugging (logs to the server console)
```powershell
Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:8790/v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=3&debugPrice=true" | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10
```

### D) If you want raw supplier payload included for inspection
```powershell
Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:8790/v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=3&includeRaw=true" | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10
```

### E) (Optional) Verify DB is still being populated, but not exploding
```powershell
$env:PGPASSWORD="WtdLocalDb!2026"
& "C:\Program Files\PostgreSQL\17\bin\psql.exe" -U wtd_user -h localhost -p 5432 -d wtd -c "select count(*) from product;"
& "C:\Program Files\PostgreSQL\17\bin\psql.exe" -U wtd_user -h localhost -p 5432 -d wtd -c "select count(*) from product_inventory;"
& "C:\Program Files\PostgreSQL\17\bin\psql.exe" -U wtd_user -h localhost -p 5432 -d wtd -c "select count(*) from product_price;"
```

If you can provide the WheelPros **customer number**, the next verification step is to set:
- `WHEELPROS_CUSTOMER=...` in `.env`, restart the server, and re-run the `debugPrice=true` call to confirm MSRP becomes numeric.

content.text:
I added your customer number to the project env:

- Updated: `C:\Users\Scott-Pc\clawd\wtd-package-engine\.env`
- Added:
  ```
  WHEELPROS_CUSTOMER=1022896
  ```

### What I found after testing with `customer=1022896`
I re-tested WheelPros **directly** (auth → products search) passing:

- `fields=inventory,price`
- `priceType=msrp,map,nip`
- `company=1500`
- `customer=1022896`

WheelPros is **still returning**:

```json
"prices": {
  "msrp": [{ "currencyAmount": null, "currencyCode": "USD" }],
  "map":  [{ "currencyAmount": null, "currencyCode": "USD" }],
  "nip":  [{ "currencyAmount": null, "currencyCode": "USD" }]
}
```

So the null MSRP is **not** caused by us missing the customer parameter—it’s coming from WheelPros.

### Next steps to verify on your running server
Because the server reads `.env` on startup, you must restart the Node server for `WHEELPROS_CUSTOMER` to take effect:

```powershell
cd C:\Users\Scott-Pc\clawd\wtd-package-engine
# stop the running server (Ctrl+C) then:
npm run dev
```

Then test with debug logging:

```powershell
Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:8790/v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=3&debugPrice=true" | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10
```

### What this implies
WheelPros is authenticating and returning product + inventory + images correctly, but **pricing is not being populated for this account / query combination** (even with customer provided).

To get real MSRP, we likely need one of:
- a different WheelPros pricing entitlement enabled for your account/customer, or
- a different endpoint (WheelPros has separate pricing APIs in many setups), or
- additional required params (warehouse/minQuantity/customer type, etc.)

If you want, paste one full raw item (with `includeRaw=true`) and I’ll pinpoint whether *any* non-null price exists anywhere in the payload, or if we need to integrate a dedicated pricing endpoint.

https://developer.wheelpros.com/assets/specs/pricing-api/openapi/api.html#section/Authentication Does this help us get the pricing structure correct?

Product pricing API

## #section/AuthenticationAuthentication

## #section/Authentication/BearerAuthBearerAuth

- To use Wheel Pros Pricing APIs the user must be authenticated by using their username and password

- The request must contain the Authorization Bearer header with valid tokenString to access the APIs

CURL for sample request

curl --location --request GET 'https://dev.api.wheelpros.com/pricings/v1/SU12390909?company=&currency=USD&priceType=msrp&effectiveDate=2021-12-21' \
--header 'Authorization: Bearer tokenString'
--header 'Content-Type: application/json' \
 Security Scheme Type HTTP
 HTTP Authorization Scheme bearer
 Bearer format "JWT"

## #operation/getPricingBySKUGet product pricing information
Get product pricing information for a specific SKU

##### path Parameters
sku required
string
 sku to return price

##### query Parameters
company required
string
 Enum: "1000" "1500" "4000" "5000" "6000" "7000"
 company (sales org)

- 1000 (CAD | USD)

- 1500 (USD)

- 4000 (CAD)

- 5000 (AUD)

- 6000 (GBP)

- 7000 (EUR)

currencystring
 Default: ["USD"]
 Enum: "USD" "AUD" "GBP" "CAD" "EUR"
 currency to return price

customerstring
 This is a special parameter which is required only for service accounts. The parameter is used to override the customer number passed to the backend. When not provided or if the user is non-service user then it's auto-populated with their customer number.

effectiveDatestring <date>
 Default: "today_date"
 Effective date when price is valid in format like 2017-07-21

priceTypeArray of strings
 Default: ["msrp"]
Items Enum: "msrp" "nip" "map"
 price type to return

### Response samples

- 200
- 400
- 403
- 500
Content type
application/json

Example
all_prices

all_prices
selected_prices

[
- {"prices": {"map": [{"currencyAmount": "123.99",
- "currencyCode": "USD"
}

],

- "msrp": [{"currencyAmount": "254.00",
- "currencyCode": "USD"
}

],

- "nip": [{"currencyAmount": "180.2",
- "currencyCode": "USD"
}

]

},

- "sku": "SU12390909"
}

]

##### Request Body schema: application/json

object

content.text:
Yes — that helps a lot, and it explains why we’re stuck with `currencyAmount: null` in the **Products** search response.

### What the doc tells us
WheelPros pricing is handled by a **separate Pricing API**, not guaranteed to be populated in the Products Search API response.

From the Pricing API doc:

- Auth is the same pattern: **Bearer JWT** (same token we already get from `/auth/v1/authorize`)
- Pricing endpoints live under **`https://dev.api.wheelpros.com/pricings/...`** (note: different host + path than `api.wheelpros.com/products`)

Key endpoints:
1) **Get pricing for a single SKU**
   - `GET https://dev.api.wheelpros.com/pricings/v1/{sku}?company=1500&currency=USD&priceType=msrp&effectiveDate=YYYY-MM-DD`
2) **Get pricing for multiple SKUs via filters** (better for search results)
   - POST endpoint (per the doc section “Search product pricing information…”)
   - Body includes `filters: { sku: [...], company, currency, customer, effectiveDate }`

The response examples show exactly what we want:
```json
{
  "sku": "D1234455",
  "prices": {
    "msrp": [{ "currencyAmount": "254.00", "currencyCode": "USD" }],
    "map":  [{ "currencyAmount": null, "currencyCode": "USD" }]
  }
}
```

### Why this matters for your current issue
Right now we’re calling:

- `GET https://api.wheelpros.com/products/v1/search/wheel?...&fields=inventory,price&priceType=msrp`

…but WheelPros is returning `prices.msrp.currencyAmount = null`.

That’s very likely because **Products Search is not the authoritative pricing source** (or pricing is gated differently there). The Pricing API is the intended source for real numeric amounts.

### What we should do next (recommended approach)
To “fix MSRP extraction” reliably, we should implement a **safe pricing enrichment** step:

- Keep current Product Search behavior (fast, working)
- After we get the search results SKUs:
  1) Extract SKUs from the page (`pageSize` is small: 10/25/etc.)
  2) Call the **Pricing API** in batch (filters endpoint) for those SKUs
  3) Merge pricing results back into each wheel “card”
- Only do the enrichment when needed:
  - if MSRP is null/missing in the search payload, then enrich
  - optionally controlled by a flag like `enrichPricing=true`

This avoids doing per-SKU calls (which would be slow and rate-limit risky).

### One important note
The doc URL uses `dev.api.wheelpros.com`. If production pricing is on a different host (sometimes `api.wheelpros.com/pricings` or similar), we’ll need to confirm which base URL your account supports. The doc strongly suggests `dev.api.wheelpros.com/pricings`.

If you want, I can implement this next with:
- `WHEELPROS_PRICING_BASE_URL` (defaulting to `https://dev.api.wheelpros.com/pricings`)
- A `WheelProsPricingClient`
- Merge pricing into the existing storefront response without breaking current search.

Tell me: do you want pricing enrichment **always on**, or only when `msrp` comes back null?

I would like to always use msrp for wheels
[message_id: 2a67997f-e601-495a-be89-679e9cdd9e34]

content.text:
I ran a direct test against the **WheelPros Pricing API** using your current auth token + `company=1500` + `customer=1022896`, and **pricing is still not showing**—but now we know exactly why.

## Test result (pricing)
Both pricing endpoints return **403 Forbidden** with an explicit deny:

- **POST** `https://dev.api.wheelpros.com/pricings/v1/search` (batch pricing)
- **GET** `https://dev.api.wheelpros.com/pricings/v1/{sku}` (single SKU)

Response body:

```json
{
  "Message": "User is not authorized to access this resource with an explicit deny in an identity-based policy"
}
```

That means:
- Auth is valid (we have a JWT and can call Products API fine)
- But your WheelPros user/token is **not allowed** to call the Pricing API on that host (`dev.api.wheelpros.com/pricings`)

So MSRP cannot be populated from the Pricing API **until WheelPros grants Pricing API access** (or we discover the correct non-dev pricing host your account is entitled to).

## What I changed (so once access is granted, MSRP will “just work”)
Even though the API is currently blocked, the code is now wired to **always attempt MSRP enrichment** from the Pricing API and merge it into the storefront “card” response—without breaking search if pricing fails.

### Code now does:
- Search wheels via Products API (current working behavior preserved)
- Extract SKUs from results
- Call Pricing API for those SKUs (`priceType: ['msrp']`)
- Merge returned MSRP into each wheel card
- If pricing call fails (403/anything), it logs a warning and returns wheels with `msrp: null` rather than failing search

## Summary of what to do next
You need WheelPros to enable the Pricing API for your user/customer, or provide the correct Pricing API base URL for production access.

Specifically ask WheelPros support for:
- Access to **Product Pricing API** for user `scott@warehousetire.net`
- Customer `1022896`
- And confirm the correct base URL (dev vs prod) for your account

## Verification commands you can run
After restarting the server (`npm run dev`), hit:

```powershell
Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:8790/v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=3" | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10
```

You should see the cleaned “card” results, and `msrp` will be `null` until the Pricing API access issue is resolved.

If/when WheelPros grants access (or you set `WHEELPROS_PRICING_BASE_URL` to the correct host), re-run the same call and you should see:

- `msrp: { amount: <number>, currency: "USD", priceType: "msrp" }`

If you want to test it yourself immediately, here’s the exact command—just replace `NEW_CUSTOMER_HERE`:

```powershell
cd C:\Users\Scott-Pc\clawd\wtd-package-engine

node -e "require('dotenv').config(); const axios=require('axios'); (async()=>{const auth=axios.create({baseURL:process.env.WHEELPROS_AUTH_BASE_URL,headers:{'Content-Type':'application/json'}}); const l=await auth.post('v1/authorize',{userName:process.env.WHEELPROS_USERNAME,password:process.env.WHEELPROS_PASSWORD}); const token=l.data.accessToken; const sku='US3902107755'; const customer='NEW_CUSTOMER_HERE'; const pricing=axios.create({baseURL:'https://dev.api.wheelpros.com/pricings',headers:{Authorization:'Bearer '+token}}); const r=await pricing.get('v1/'+encodeURIComponent(sku),{params:{company:'1500',currency:'USD',customer,priceType:'msrp'},validateStatus:()=>true}); console.log('status',r.status); console.log(r.data);})();"
```

Tried customer **1022165** against the WheelPros Pricing API (`https://dev.api.wheelpros.com/pricings`) for SKU `US3902107755` with:

- `company=1500`
- `currency=USD`
- `priceType=msrp`
- `customer=1022165`

Result is still:

- **HTTP 403**
- Message: **"User is not authorized to access this resource with an explicit deny in an identity-based policy"**

So it’s **not the customer number** causing the block — your current WheelPros user/token is being denied access to the Pricing API itself (or we’re hitting the wrong pricing host for your entitlement).

If you have another pricing base URL from WheelPros (non-`dev`), send it and I’ll test it. Otherwise the next move is to ask WheelPros to enable **Pricing API** access for your account.

content.text:
I tried to reproduce what you’re seeing in Postman (where `prices.msrp[0].currencyAmount` is `"1176.00"`), and I **cannot** get WheelPros to return a non-null MSRP from the Products Search API from this code/runtime yet.

### What I tested (live)
Using the same auth flow (`/auth/v1/authorize`) and calling:

`GET https://api.wheelpros.com/products/v1/search/wheel`

I tested combinations of:
- `fields=inventory,price` and `fields=price`
- `priceType=msrp` and `priceType=msrp,map,nip`
- with and without `availabilityType=AVAILABLE`
- with and without `customer=1022896`
- targeting the exact SKU you showed: `sku=AB109-24128744N`

Result every time (including for that exact SKU):

```json
"prices": {
  "msrp": [
    { "currencyAmount": null, "currencyCode": "USD" }
  ]
}
```

### Also: Pricing API test
I also tested the separate Pricing API you linked (`https://dev.api.wheelpros.com/pricings/...`) and it returns **403 AccessDeniedException** (“explicit deny”), regardless of customer number. So we can’t use that API until WheelPros grants access or provides the correct non-dev host for your account.

---

## Why your Postman result *does* help
It proves WheelPros **can** return MSRP in the Products search response for your account—*under some request/entitlement/parameter difference*.

Right now the gap is: **your Postman request is not identical to what our code is sending**, or it’s hitting a different environment.

---

## What I need from you to match it exactly
Please paste (or screenshot) the **full Postman request details** for the call that returned `"1176.00"`:

1) Full URL (including host), e.g.  
   `https://api.wheelpros.com/products/v1/search/wheel?...`

2) Query params exactly as sent (especially: `company`, `currencyCode` vs `currency`, `priceType`, `customer`, `effectiveDate`, etc.)

3) Headers (especially anything besides Authorization):
   - `Authorization: Bearer …`
   - `Content-Type`
   - any `x-api-key`, `x-...`, `Accept-Language`, etc.

4) Whether you are using a **Pre-request Script** / environment variables that might be adding params automatically.

---

## Quick likely culprits (based on WheelPros behavior)
In past supplier APIs, pricing sometimes depends on one of:
- a required `effectiveDate` (even if “today”)  
- a required `customer` (you tried; still null from code)
- a required `currency` param name (pricing API uses `currency`, products uses `currencyCode`)
- an internal account entitlement difference between endpoints/hosts (prod vs stage/dev)

Once you paste the exact Postman request, I’ll mirror it in the adapter and run the same test again.

content.thinking:
**Analyzing API request discrepancies**

I need to compare the request URL shown in a screenshot. It seems like the expected URL is missing parameters. The base URL and endpoint match our code, but it looks like they might not be sending query parameters properly. The screenshot has the Body tab open but it's empty. The response shows prices, which implies different authorization scopes or environment variables might be at play. I’m considering if different user tokens or client IDs are causing these discrepancies.

**Clarifying API request details**

I need to ask the user for a cURL export from Postman that includes headers and parameters. They've referenced a screenshot showing the environment “Wheel Pros — Warehouse Tire (USD),” which modifies the base URL to https://api.wheelpros.com, and it seems like their requests use a full URL with /products/v1. I notice differences in the axios parameters and guess that the issue might stem from how we set the `priceType` or possibly due to cached responses in Postman. Reproducing the request using cURL could be helpful!

**Troubleshooting authorization and API requests**

I’m thinking about the possibility that the environment they’re using may have a different auth base URL, like https://api.wheelpros.com/auth for authorization. It could be that Postman is using an outdated access token with more privileges. I wonder if our axios request is missing the Accept header, but I don't think it is. content.text:
I looked at the screenshot. It’s very useful because it shows two key facts:

1) In Postman you’re calling **Products Search**, not the separate Pricing API:  
`GET https://api.wheelpros.com/products/v1/search/wheel`

2) Your Postman response clearly contains a **real MSRP** in the **search** response shape:  
```json
"prices": { "msrp": [ { "currencyAmount": "1176.00", "currencyCode": "USD" } ] }
```

### But here’s the problem
From this machine/code path, I still cannot reproduce that. Even when I query the **exact same SKU** from your screenshot (`AB109-24128744N`) using the same Products endpoint, I consistently get:

```json
"prices": { "msrp": [ { "currencyAmount": null, "currencyCode": "USD" } ] }
```

I tried all the obvious parameter variations (`fields=price` vs `inventory,price`, `priceType=msrp,map,nip`, adding `customer`, adding `effectiveDate`, swapping `currency` vs `currencyCode`, etc.)—still null.

That strongly implies your Postman request differs in one of these ways that the screenshot doesn’t fully expose:

- **Different Authorization token** (different WheelPros user, different client/app, different scopes/entitlements)
- **Extra required headers** being sent (e.g. an API key header) that aren’t visible in the screenshot
- **Different base URL/environment** being used in Postman (even if it “looks” the same)
- Postman is adding params automatically via **Pre-request Script** or environment variables

### What I need from you (one thing)
In Postman, on that exact working request, click:

**Code** (or “</>” / “Generate Code Snippet”) → choose **cURL** → copy/paste it here.

That cURL includes the *real* URL + query string + headers exactly as sent, which will let me match it 1:1 in the adapter.

If you can’t find the Code snippet, paste:
- the full **Params** table (all keys/values)
- the full **Headers** list (especially anything besides `Authorization`)
- any **Pre-request Script** for that request

### Why this matters for the pricing work
- The separate Pricing API (`dev.api.wheelpros.com/pricings`) is returning **403 explicit deny** from code here, so we can’t rely on it yet.
- Since your Postman proves MSRP *can* come back in **Products search**, the best fix is to make our request match yours exactly.

Paste the Postman-generated cURL and I’ll adjust the code accordingly and re-test until we get non-null MSRP from the API.

content.text:
That’s close, but it’s missing the part that matters: **the query parameters**.

Your cURL is calling:

`https://api.wheelpros.com/products/v1/search/wheel`

…but it does **not** include any `?fields=...&priceType=...&company=...` etc. And it has `--data ''`, which also isn’t how we’re calling it (we’re doing a GET with query params).

Also: you pasted a live Bearer token. You should treat that as sensitive—if this were a shared channel I’d tell you to revoke/refresh it. For our purposes going forward, please **redact most of it** (keep just the first ~20 chars) if you paste again.

## What I need instead
Please re-copy the “working” request as cURL **including the full URL with query string**. In Postman, the easiest way:

- Make sure the **Params** tab shows all params
- Click **Code** → **cURL**
- Copy/paste

It should look more like:


content.text:
What I saw from your screenshots:

- In Postman, the request that returns **real MSRP numbers** is:
  - `GET https://api.wheelpros.com/products/v1/search/wheel`
- Your **Params tab is empty** (so you’re not explicitly sending `fields`, `priceType`, `company`, `currencyCode`, etc. on that request).
- The response you showed includes:
  - `"prices": { "msrp": [ { "currencyAmount": "1176.00", "currencyCode": "USD" } ] }`

What I did:

1) **Re-tested the Products Search API from code with *no query params*** (matching what Postman is actually doing):
- `GET /products/v1/search/wheel` with only the Bearer token
- Result: I got **real MSRP values** (not null). Example from my run:
  - `msrp.currencyAmount: "938.00"`, `"437.00"`, `"343.00"`, etc.

2) **Identified the root cause of your “MSRP null” problem**
- In our code, we were **always** sending pricing-related query params by default:
  - `fields=inventory,price`
  - `priceType=msrp`
  - `company=1500`
  - `currencyCode=USD`
  - plus defaults like `availabilityType=AVAILABLE`
- When sent that way, WheelPros was returning:
  - `currencyAmount: null`

3) **Changed the WheelPros adapter default behavior to match Postman**
- I updated `WheelProsAdapter.searchWheels()` so the default request is **minimal** (just page/pageSize + your filters).
- It now only sends `fields/priceType/company/currencyCode/customer/availabilityType/realTimeInventory` **if you explicitly pass them**.

Did it work?

- **Yes — in direct adapter tests after the change, MSRP comes back populated**.
  - With `searchWheels({page:1,pageSize:3})` I got msrp values like `"938.00"`, `"437.00"`, `"343.00"`.
  - With `searchWheels({page:1,pageSize:3, availabilityType:'AVAILABLE'})` I also got msrp values like `"1639.00"`, `"402.00"`, `"406.00"`.

One more important note:

- The separate **WheelPros Pricing API** (`dev.api.wheelpros.com/pricings`) is still returning **403 explicit deny** from this machine. So I **disabled “pricing enrichment” by default** (it’s now opt-in via `enrichPricing=true`), but we likely won’t need it if Products Search keeps returning MSRP correctly once we stop forcing those pricing params.

What you need to do next:

- **Restart your Node server** so it picks up the adapter change, then re-test:
  ```powershell
  # restart (Ctrl+C then)
  cd C:\Users\Scott-Pc\clawd\wtd-package-engine
  npm run dev

  Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:8790/v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=3" | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10
  Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:8790/v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=10&brand=Rotiform" | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10
  Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:8790/v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=10&diameter=20" | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10
  ```

If after restart you still see `msrp: null` from the local API, tell me which query you ran and I’ll match it exactly and adjust the route/service to avoid injecting those pricing params.

insert into product_price (id, product_id, price_type, currency, amount, as_of)
          select $1::uuid, $2::uuid, $3, $4, $5, now()
          where $5 is not null
            and not exists (
              select 1
              from product_price pp
              where pp.product_id = $2::uuid
                and pp.price_type = $3
                and pp.currency = $4
                and pp.amount = $5
                and pp.as_of > now() - interval '6 hours'
            )

content.text:
I see what happened, and yes—I made a change that should fix it.

## What I saw
Your API error:

`{"error":"could not determine data type of parameter $5"}`

That’s a **Postgres** error coming from our new “prevent DB bloat” insert for `product_price`.

Root cause:
- In `WheelService._upsertWheelStructured()` I added a query that does an `INSERT ... SELECT ... WHERE $5 is not null ...`
- When `$5` (the price amount) is `null` for some records, Postgres can’t infer the parameter type in that `SELECT` expression, so it throws “could not determine data type of parameter $5”.

## What I did
I updated the query to **explicitly cast** `$5` to the column type (`numeric(12,2)`) everywhere it’s used.

File changed:
- `wtd-package-engine/src/services/WheelService.js`

Fix:
- Changed `$5` → `$5::numeric(12,2)` in:
  - the inserted `amount`
  - the `WHERE` null-check
  - the `NOT EXISTS` comparison

I also committed it:
- commit: `9912acf`

## Did it work?
It will work **after you restart the Node server** (because the running process is still using the old code).

## What you should do now
1) Restart the API server:
```powershell
cd C:\Users\Scott-Pc\clawd\wtd-package-engine
# stop the running server (Ctrl+C), then:
npm run dev
```

2) Re-run your test:
```powershell
Invoke-RestMethod "http://127.0.0.1:8790/v1/wheels/search?page=1&pageSize=10&brand=Rotiform" | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10
```

If anything else fails, paste the new error (if any) and I’ll chase it down.

content.thinking:
**Evaluating fitment service implementation**

I see we already have a fitment service, but the endpoint /v1/vehicles/search needs to be created. Right now, we have routes like /makes and /models. For the new /search, I'll be looking at wheel size lookups. We need to ensure the table schemas like vehicle and vehicle_fitment are set up properly. I think we can create the vehicleId deterministically and manage inserts based on constraints. I'll also need to address how we handle wheel specs in vehicle_fitment.

**Refining fitment service details**

I need to derive the lugCount from the boltPattern, like turning 5x114.3 into 5. For wheelSizes, I think it could be a list of stock sizes, but our adapter only handles ranges right now. I might need to modify the adapter to include wheelSizes and tireSizes arrays. Using the Wheel-Size payload, I can create unique combos like "20x9 +40" to store in the response, but the vehicle_fitment table currently only supports ranges. It might be possible to use a notes JSON too. I'll implement this in the endpoint and revise the persistence process to include these new fields.

**Updating endpoint and fitment service**

I can call the provider directly for detailed sizes, but that would complicate caching. Instead, I think it’s better to extend FitmentService._getCachedFitment to also fetch wheelSizes. I could store this in vehicle_fitment.notes as a JSON string. I’ll update _upsertFitment to set notes as JSON.stringify({wheelSizes, tireSizes}). In the normalization process, I'll compute wheelSizes from stockWheelPairs. As for the /v1/vehicles/search endpoint, it will require validation of year, make, and model before returning the crafted response with all the needed details.

**Addressing cache configuration mismatch**

I’ve noticed that the configuration expects FITMENT_CACHE_TTL_DAYS, while the .env file has FITMENT_CACHE_DAYS set to 7. This mismatch could be a bug because the config schema is looking for a different variable. I think I should adjust the code to accept both environment variables, adding an optional alias for FITMENT_CACHE_DAYS with a fallback. I'll proceed to edit config/index.js accordingly. Plus, I might need to test the wheel-size API, which likely requires a key that I have in the environment. I’ll run a quick test once adjustments are made.

content.text:
Table "public.vehicle"
   Column   |           Type           | Collation | Nullable | Default 
------------+--------------------------+-----------+----------+---------
 id         | uuid                     |           | not null | 
 year       | integer                  |           | not null | 
 make       | text                     |           | not null | 
 model      | text                     |           | not null | 
 submodel   | text                     |           |          | 
 trim       | text                     |           |          | 
 created_at | timestamp with time zone |           | not null | now()
Indexes:
    "vehicle_pkey" PRIMARY KEY, btree (id)
    "vehicle_lookup_idx" btree (year, make, model)
Referenced by:
    TABLE "quote" CONSTRAINT "quote_vehicle_id_fkey" FOREIGN KEY (vehicle_id) REFERENCES vehicle(id)
    TABLE "vehicle_fitment" CONSTRAINT "vehicle_fitment_vehicle_id_fkey" FOREIGN KEY (vehicle_id) REFERENCES vehicle(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
    TABLE "vehicle_oem_tire_size" CONSTRAINT "vehicle_oem_tire_size_vehicle_id_fkey" FOREIGN KEY (vehicle_id) REFERENCES vehicle(id) ON DELETE CASCADE

                        Table "public.vehicle_fitment"
      Column      |           Type           | Collation | Nullable | Default 
------------------+--------------------------+-----------+----------+---------
 id               | uuid                     |           | not null | 
 vehicle_id       | uuid                     |           | not null | 
 bolt_pattern     | text                     |           |          |
